2: The man caught up to the third heaven / Paradise (2 Corinthians 12:2-4): Paul's apocalyptic-vision tradition belongs to the well-documented Second Temple Jewish literature of heavenly ascent — 1 Enoch, 2 Enoch, 3 Baruch, T. Levi, and later the Hekhalot literature describe journeys through multiple heavens (three, five, or seven, depending on the source). The rabbinic concept of pardes (paradise) as the garden of God in the highest heaven (b. Hagigah 14b — the four who entered pardes) provides context. Paul's reticence (he cannot tell whether in the body or out of the body, 2 Corinthians 12:2-3; he refuses to boast about it, 2 Corinthians 12:5) is the inverse of the typical apocalyptic visionary who boasts of his heavenly journey.
1: The transition from the escape narrative to the heavenly ascent: 'I must go on boasting — although there is nothing to be gained, I will go on to visions and revelations.' The heavenly ascent (2 Corinthians 12:2-4) is a genre of Jewish apocalyptic literature. 1 Enoch, 2 Enoch, the Testament of Levi 2-3, and 4 Ezra all feature heavenly journeys in which the seer ascends through multiple heavens and receives divine revelations. By the first century, multiple-heaven cosmologies were well established in Jewish thought. Paul's account is notably reticent: he does not report what he saw or heard.
3: The double statement of ignorance — 'whether in the body or apart from the body I do not know, but God knows' — repeats for emphasis. Paul cannot determine whether the experience was visionary (a trance-like state) or actual bodily transport. Both possibilities were live options in Jewish apocalyptic: some traditions described the visionary as transported bodily (1 Enoch 2 Corinthians 39:3), others as experiencing an ecstatic out-of-body state. Paul's agnosticism here is itself significant — the experience was so overwhelming that ordinary categories of embodied/disembodied consciousness did not apply.
4: Paradise (paradeisos): the Persian loanword (pairi-daeza = enclosed garden) appears in the LXX for the Garden of Eden (Genesis 2:8-10) and for the eschatological garden of God (Isaiah 51:3; Ezekiel 28:13; 2 Corinthians 31:8-9). In intertestamental literature, paradise became the realm of the righteous dead or the eschatological reward. The Talmud (b.Hagigah 14b) records the 'four who entered pardes' — Ben Azzai, Ben Zoma, Acher, and Akiba — a rabbinic tradition about mystical ascent that may echo the same traditions Paul draws on. 'Inexpressible things' (arreta rhemata) — things not permitted to speak: the concealment of heavenly secrets is standard in Jewish apocalyptic (1 Enoch 2 Corinthians 14:3; Revelation 10:4).
5: The deliberate self-distancing: 'I will boast about that person, but not about myself, except about my weaknesses.' Paul speaks of the heavenly-ascent experience in third person ('a man in Christ') — a rhetorical distancing that signals this is not normal Sophistic self-promotion. The ascent is real, but Paul will not leverage it the way a Sophistic wonder-worker would. The rival missionaries apparently emphasized visionary experience and spiritual power as credentials; Paul relativizes the ascent experience entirely by immediately pivoting to the thorn (2 Corinthians 12:7).
6: Paul's reason for restraint in heavenly boasting: so no one will think more of me than what they observe (blepei me e akouei ex emou — sees or hears from me). This is the anti-Sophistic epistemology of 2 Corinthians: apostolic credibility comes from observable conduct and audible message, not from claimed supernatural experiences that cannot be verified. The visible Paul — weak, persecuted, free-preaching — is the Paul to be evaluated. The heavenly visionary Paul must be bracketed for the sake of honest apostolic relationship.
7: The thorn in the flesh (skolops te sarki): skolops can mean a stake, a pointed instrument, a splinter, or a thorn. The phrase 'messenger of Satan' (angelos Satana) suggests either a demonic agent of affliction or a person who was Satan's instrument in harassing Paul. Identifications proposed by modern scholars include: ophthalmia (eye disease, suggested by Galatians 4:13-15 where Paul implies the Galatians would have given him their eyes), epilepsy, malaria, a speech impediment, sexual temptation, or severe depression. The ambiguity is probably intentional — Paul does not want the thorn identified with a specific condition but understood as a structural feature of apostolic weakness.
8: Three times Paul appealed to the Lord (ton Kyrion parekalesa): the triple petition echoes Jesus' Gethsemane prayer (Matthew 26:36-44: three times Jesus prayed for the cup to pass; it did not). The pattern of unanswered petition followed by divine reassurance of sufficient grace is a defining moment in Paul's theology: prayer is not a mechanism for removing divine-permitted suffering but an encounter with the God who redeems suffering by inhabiting it.
9: The divine response: 'my grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness' (he dynamis mou en astheneia teleitai). This is one of the rare direct divine speech-acts in Paul's letters. The structure — grace sufficient, power perfected in weakness — reverses the Sophistic credentials: where rivals demonstrate power through strength, God demonstrates power through Paul's weakness. The thorn is not removed because the unanswered prayer, the ongoing weakness, is the very locus of divine power-display. Paul's subsequent 'boasting in weaknesses' is theologically grounded here.
10: The delight in weaknesses (astheneia), insults (hybris), hardships (anagkais), persecutions (diogmois), difficulties (stenochoriois) — a five-item peristasis variation. Hybris (insults, arrogant mistreatment) was a legal category in Athenian and Roman law: deliberate degradation of a person's honor. That Paul lists it alongside physical hardships shows the social-honor dimension of apostolic suffering — not just pain but public dishonor. The closing principle: 'when I am weak, then I am strong' (hotan gar asthenoo, tote dynatos eimi) — the paradox that defines the Fool's Speech entire.
11: 'You drove me to it' (hymeis me enagkasate): the Corinthians' failure to defend Paul against the rivals forced the Fool's Speech. The community's passivity in the face of the rivals' attacks constituted an implicit endorsement of the Sophistic evaluation criteria. 'I am not in the least inferior to the super-apostles, even though I am nothing' (oudenos hystereka ton hyperlian apostolon, ei kai ouden eimi): the double move — superior and nothing. The rhetorical comparison paradox: Paul beats the rivals at their own game of comparison while simultaneously declaring that the comparison game itself is worthless.
12: The signs of an apostle (semeia tou apostolou): the community is the proof. Signs, wonders, and miracles (semeiois te kai terasin kai dynamesin) were performed among them. The triple formula for miraculous works (cf. Acts 2:22 of Jesus; Hebrews 2:4) was the recognized evidential basis for apostolic claims in the early church. Paul acknowledges this criterion — the rivals apparently emphasized it — while placing it firmly in the past and pointing to suffering and weakness as the present evidence of genuine apostolicity.
13: The ironic 'wrong' of not accepting payment: 'how were you inferior to the other churches, except that I was never a burden to you? Forgive me this wrong' (charisasthe moi ten adikian tauten). The sarcasm is pointed. In the patronage economy, a teacher who refused payment from a wealthy patron was depriving that patron of the opportunity to display their generosity and social status. By refusing Corinthian support, Paul was arguably insulting them by denying them the honor of being his benefactors. The ironic apology acknowledges this cultural dynamic while maintaining the policy.
14: Third visit announced: this confirms that a second, painful visit (mentioned in 2 Corinthians 2:1) occurred between 1 and 2 Corinthians. Paul's apostolic relationship to the community is familial: parents save up for children, not the reverse. The Roman family was the basic unit of economic provision: patria potestas gave the father authority and responsibility over the household. Paul inverts the Sophistic economic relationship (teacher charges clients) with the parental model: he expends himself for them as a father for children.
15: 'I will very gladly spend and be entirely spent (dapaneso kai ekdapanethisomai) for your lives.' The double verb is intensifying: spend (resources) and be spent (self). The voluntary self-expenditure language echoes 2 Corinthians 4:12's 'death is at work in us, life in you' — the apostle as the one who is consumed so that the community may live. The rhetorical question 'if I love you more, will you love me less' expresses genuine pastoral vulnerability — Paul is not cynically performing love but genuinely wounded by the asymmetry.
16: The accusation Paul addresses: even though he accepted no payment, he was crafty (panourgos) and caught them by trickery (doloo). The charge may be that Paul received support through Titus or others while maintaining the appearance of financial independence. Panourgos (all-working, unscrupulously clever) is the adjective form of panourgia (cunning, 2 Corinthians 11:3; 2 Corinthians 4:2) — the very quality Paul attributed to the serpent and the false apostles. Now his accusers turn the same word back on him.
17: The rhetorical question about Titus and the other brother: did Paul exploit the Corinthians through his envoys? The question expects a negative answer — both Titus and the unnamed brother behaved with integrity. This defends against the charge of 2 Corinthians 12:16: if Paul were exploiting through agents, his agents would have shown it. The parallel conduct of Paul and Titus in financial matters (to auto pneumati periepatesamen) is offered as evidence of consistent apostolic integrity.
18: Titus and the anonymous brother walked 'in the same footsteps' (tois autois ichnesin): the metaphor of following in someone's tracks or steps (ichnos) was used for disciples following a teacher's way of life. Paul and his co-workers constitute a school of apostolic conduct characterized by non-exploitation and free service. This consistency across multiple persons is evidence against the accusation of covert manipulation.
19: 'Have you been thinking all along that we have been defending ourselves to you?' The meta-commentary: the Fool's Speech was not a legal apologia (defense before a court) but an act of apostolic construction (oikodome). The community is the audience before whom Paul speaks, not the judge. 'We have been speaking in the sight of God as those in Christ' — the defense is oriented toward divine accountability, not human verdict. The purpose of the speech was their building up, not his acquittal.
20: The fear of finding the community in disorder: a vice catalogue (quarreling, jealousy, anger, selfishness, slander, gossip, conceit, disorder — eris, zelos, thymos, eritheia, katalalia, psithyrismos, physiosis, akatastasiai). This list of eight social vices maps the dysfunction of a community shaped by Sophistic patronage competition. The rival missionaries' presence has not improved Corinthian community life; it has intensified the status-competition and social fragmentation that Paul's first letter already addressed.
21: The feared personal humiliation: 'my God will humble me before you' (tapeinosei me ho theos mou). The mourning over those who have sinned (proemartekoton) and not repented suggests specific known members of the community who engaged in sexual immorality (aselgeia, porneia, akatharsia). The Corinthian sexual ethics problem (1 Cor 5-7) apparently persisted despite the earlier letter's instructions. Paul's grief is pastoral, not punitive: he mourns over the unrepentant before he confronts them.
